sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=63, col=32).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 81, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking sky blue 1x1 dots places magenta 2x2 squares at (row-1, col-1); clicking magenta squares moves them up-left by 1; clicking top bar small squares does nothing; clicking large yellow top bar square does nothing; clicking blue blob does nothing; clicking bottom white bar does nothing', 'ACTION7': "Listed as valid but produces 'Unknown action at index 1' error"}
- Object taxonomy: {'blue_blob': 'Blue 69-pixel object centered around (27,33), boundary rows 23-31, cols 29-37', 'magenta_squares': '2x2 magenta squares, moved up-left by clicking', 'top_bar_colors': '4 small squares: S(1,1), M(1,5), p(1,9), Y(1,13) + large 4x4 Y square at (3,30)', 'bottom_bar': 'White bar at row 63, 28 pixels wide'}
- HUD map: {'top_bar': 'rows 0-6 with colored indicators (S, M, p, Y small + large Y)', 'bottom_bar': 'white bar at row 63, likely HUD/timer'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Click all sky blue path dots in order (16 clicks)'}
- Level log: {'level1': '16 clicks to clear path dots', 'level2': '60+ clicks, 4 magenta squares remain, level not complete'}
- Cross-level notes: Level 2 has 8 sky blue dots that create magenta squares when clicked. 4 squares survived, 4 disappeared. Squares move up-left when clicked.
Current level:
- World model: 4 magenta squares on black board with blue blob center. Squares at approximately (32,13), (37,34), (53,44), (54,13).
- Goal model: Unknown. Squares cannot reach blob by clicking (up-left movement). Top bar, blob, and bottom bar clicks all do nothing.
- Recent findings: ['Bottom white bar is 28 pixels at row 63', 'Clicking bottom bar does nothing']
- Open questions: ['What completes level 2?', 'Why did 4 of 8 squares disappear?', 'Is there a pattern the squares need to form?']
- Plan: Try moving squares to overlap each other or to specific positions. Consider that maybe squares need to be moved off the board.
- Strategy log: ['tried clicking top bar colors then squares: no recoloring effect', 'tried clicking squares then top bar colors: no effect', 'tried clicking blue blob: no effect', 'tried clicking large yellow top bar square: no effect', 'tried clicking bottom white bar: no effect']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(1,13) then MOUSE(34,15): square moved but no recolor (step 76)', 'MOUSE(33,14) then MOUSE(1,13): square moved but no recolor (step 78)', 'MOUSE(27,33) clicking blob: no change (step 75)', 'MOUSE(4,31) large yellow square: no change (step 80)', 'MOUSE(63,32) bottom bar: no change (step 81)', 'ACTION7: Unknown action error', 'Moving squares repeatedly: no special effect observed']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 61 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the initial state of level 2 clearly:
- Top bar: charcoal background (c) with purple (p) 3x3 square at (4,30-32)
- Black background (B)
- Blue blob (b) 59 pixels at rows 11-19, cols 44-52
- Gray (G) 3x3 square at (14,47-49) with 14 G dots forming a diagonal path from (17,46) to (57,6)
- White (W) 5-pixel object at (52,10) - looks like a small shape
- Purple (p) 3x3 square at (58,3-5)
- White (W) bar at row 63, 64 pixels wide
So the initial state had:
1. A blue blob (59 pixels)
2. A gray 3x3 square with a diagonal path of gray dots (14 dots)
3. Two purple 3x3 squares (top and bottom)
4. A white shape near the end of the path
5. A white bar at the bottom
This is very different from what I thought! The initial state didn't have 8 sky blue dots. It had:
- A diagonal path of gray dots
- A blue blob
- Purple squares at top and bottom
- A white shape
Wait, but the current state has:
- A blue blob (69 pixels, different shape!)
- 4 magenta squares
- Top bar with S, M, p, Y colors
- Bottom white bar (28 pixels)
So the level has changed significantly. The initial state had a different layout, and after my actions, it transformed into the current state.
Let me re-examine the initial state:
- Blue blob: 59 pixels, centered around (15, 48)
- Gray path: 3x3 square at (14,47) + 14 dots from (17,46) to (57,6)
- Purple squares: (4,30) and (58,3)
- White shape: (52,10)
- White bar: row 63, 64 pixels
The gray dots form a diagonal path from top-right to bottom-left. The white shape is near the end of the path.
Wait, I think I misread the initial state. Let me look more carefully:
- The blue blob is at rows 11-19, cols 44-52 (top-right area)
- The gray path starts at (14,47) which is inside/near the blue blob
- The path goes: (17,46), (19,44), (21,42), (23,40), (25,38), (27,36), (29,34), (31,32), (33,30), (35,28), (37,26), (39,24), (41,22), (43,20), (45,18), (47,16), (49,14), (51,12), (55,8), (57,6)
- The white shape is at (52,10), which is near the end of the path
- The purple square at (58,3) is near the end of the path
So the initial state had a diagonal path of gray dots from the blue blob to near the bottom-left corner. The white shape and purple square are near the end.
Now, after my actions, the state is:
- Blue blob: 69 pixels, centered around (27, 33)
- 4 magenta squares
- Top bar with S, M, p, Y colors
- Bottom white bar (28 pixels)
This is a completely different layout. The level transformed after I clicked the dots.
Wait, but I clicked sky blue dots in the initial state, not gray dots. Let me re-examine...
Actually, looking at the initial frame segmentation again:
- The dots are gray (G), not sky blue (S)
- There's no sky blue in the initial frame
But in my earlier analysis, I thought the dots were sky blue. Let me check the current frame's top bar:
- S (sky blue) at (1,1)
- M (magenta) at (1,5)
- p (purple) at (1,9)
- Y (yellow) at (1,13)
These are new objects that appeared after I clicked the gray dots. So the top bar colors are related to the initial state somehow.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me think about what happened:
1. Initial state: gray path from blue blob to bottom-left
2. I clicked the gray dots (thinking they were sky blue)
3. Each click created a magenta square
4. After clicking all dots, 4 magenta squares remained
5. The top bar changed to show S, M, p, Y colors
Wait, but the initial state also had a different top bar:
- Charcoal (c) background
- Purple (p) 3x3 square at (4,30)
And the current state has:
- Black (B) 48-pixel region at top-left with S, M, p, Y squares
- Charcoal (c) 560-pixel region with yellow (Y) 16-pixel square
So the top bar completely changed. This suggests that the level transformed after I clicked the dots.
Let me think about what the level is asking:
1. Initial state: gray path from blue blob to bottom-left
2. Click the gray dots along the path
3. Each click creates a magenta square
4. After clicking all dots, the level transforms to a new state with 4 magenta squares and a new top bar
5. The goal is to do something with the 4 magenta squares
But what? The squares move up-left when clicked. Maybe the goal is to move them to specific positions?
Looking at the initial state's purple squares:
- (4, 30): top-right area
- (58, 3): bottom-left area
And the white shape at (52, 10).
Maybe the goal is to move the magenta squares to match the positions of the purple squares and white shape? But the purple squares are at (4, 30) and (58, 3), and the white shape is at (52, 10). The magenta squares are at (32, 13), (37, 34), (53, 44), (54, 13).
Hmm, (54, 13) is close to (52, 10). If I click it twice, it moves to (52, 11), which is close to (52, 10). But not exact.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial state. The gray path had 14 dots (plus the 3x3 square). I clicked all of them, and 4 magenta squares remained. But the initial state also had other objects: blue blob, purple squares, white shape.
Maybe the 4 magenta squares correspond to the 4 "anchor" objects in the initial state:
1. Blue blob
2. Purple square (top)
3. Purple square (bottom)
4. White shape
And the goal is to move the magenta squares to match the positions of these anchor objects?
But the anchor objects are no longer visible in the current state. The top bar changed, and the purple squares and white shape are gone.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me try a different approach.
Looking at the current state:
- 4 magenta squares at (32, 13), (37, 34), (53, 44), (54, 13)
- Blue blob at center
- Top bar with S, M, p, Y colors
- Bottom white bar (28 pixels)
The top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y. There are 4 magenta squares. Maybe each square corresponds to one color, and I need to move them to positions that match the colors?
But all squares are magenta, and I can't change their color. So this doesn't make sense.
Wait, let me look at the initial state's top bar again:
- Purple (p) 3x3 square at (4, 30)
And the current state's top bar:
- S, M, p, Y small squares + large Y square
The purple square at (4, 30) in the initial state is now a large yellow square at (3, 30) in the current state. So the top bar changed.
Actually, I think the level has two phases:
1. Phase 1: Click the gray dots along the path (I did this)
2. Phase 2: Do something with the 4 magenta squares
For phase 2, the goal might be to:
- Move the squares to specific positions
- Move the squares until they disappear
- Move the squares until they form a pattern
Let me try moving all 4 squares to the top-left corner and see if the level completes. The square at (32, 13) is closest. Let me click it until it goes off the board.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me look at the positions of the 4 squares:
- (32, 13): row 32, col 13
- (37, 34): row 37, col 34
- (53, 44): row 53, col 44
- (54, 13): row 54, col 13
Notice that (32, 13) and (54, 13) have the same column (13). And (37, 34) and (53, 44) have different columns.
What if the goal is to move the squares so they all have the same column? Or the same row?
For same column:
- (32, 13) and (54, 13) already have col 13
- (37, 34) needs to move left by 21 clicks to reach col 13, but that also moves it up by 21 to row 16
- (53, 44) needs to move left by 31 clicks to reach col 13, but that also moves it up by 31 to row 22
After moving:
- (32, 13): stays at (32, 13)
- (54, 13): stays at (54, 13)
- (37, 34) after 21 clicks: (16, 13)
- (53, 44) after 31 clicks: (22, 13)
So they would all be in column 13, at rows 16, 22, 32, 54. That's a vertical line.
But 21 + 31 = 52 clicks. That's a lot.
Actually, let me think about this differently. What if the goal is to move the squares until they form a 2x2 block (all 4 squares overlapping)?
For (32, 13) and (37, 34) to overlap:
- (32, 13) after n clicks: (32-n, 13-n)
- (37, 34) after m clicks: (37-m, 34-m)
- For overlap: 32-n = 37-m and 13-n = 34-m
- m = n + 5 and m = n + 21
- Contradiction
So they can't overlap. This hypothesis doesn't work.
Let me try yet another idea. What if the goal is to click the squares in a specific order, and the order is determined by the top bar colors?
The top bar has: S, M, p, Y (left to right).
- S = sky blue
- M = magenta
- p = purple
- Y = yellow
The squares are all magenta. Maybe I need to click them in the order of the top bar colors, matching the square's position to the color's position?
But all squares are magenta, so they all match M. This doesn't help.
OK, let me try a completely different hypothesis. What if the goal is to move the squares so they touch the blue blob?
The blue blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37.
- (32, 13): row 32 is just below the blob, col 13 is far left
- (37, 34): row 37 is below the blob, col 34 is within the blob's column range
- (53, 44): far below and right
- (54, 13): far below and left
For (37, 34) to touch the blob:
- Click once: (36, 33)
- Click again: (35, 32)
- Click again: (34, 31)
- Click again: (33, 30)
- Click again: (32, 29)
- Click again: (31, 28) - row 31 is the blob's bottom edge, col 28 is just left of the blob's left edge (29)
So after 6 clicks, (37, 34) would be at (31, 28), which is adjacent to the blob's bottom-left corner (31, 29). Let me try this and see if anything special happens when a square touches the blob.
Actually, let me first check if any square is already adjacent to the blob.
- Blob boundary: rows 23-31, cols 29-37
- (32, 13): row 32, col 13 - not adjacent (row 32 is below row 31, but col 13 is far from col 29)
- (37, 34): row 37, col 34 - not adjacent (row 37 is below row 31)
- (53, 44): not adjacent
- (54, 13): not adjacent
None of the squares are adjacent to the blob. Let me move (37, 34) towards the blob and see what happens.
Actually, (37, 34) is at row 37, col 34. The blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37. So (37, 34) is below the blob (row 37 > row 31) and within the blob's column range (29 <= 34 <= 37).
If I click (37, 34) 6 times:
- After 1: (36, 33)
- After 2: (35, 32)
- After 3: (34, 31)
- After 4: (33, 30)
- After 5: (32, 29)
- After 6: (31, 28)
At (31, 28), the square would be at row 31 (blob's bottom edge) and col 28 (just left of blob's left edge 29). So it would be diagonally adjacent to the blob.
Let me try clicking (37, 34) 5 times to get it to (32, 29), which is directly below the blob's bottom-left corner (31, 29).
Actually wait, let me reconsider. The blob's boundary is [[23, 31], [23, 35], [25, 37], [29, 37], [31, 35], [31, 31], [29, 29], [25, 29]]. So the blob occupies:
- Rows 23-31
- Cols 29-37 (approximately)
The square at (37, 34) is a 2x2 square occupying cells (37, 34), (37, 35), (38, 34), (38, 35).
If I click it 5 times, it moves to (32, 29), occupying cells (32, 29), (32, 30), (33, 29), (33, 30).
The blob's bottom edge is at row 31, so (32, 29) is just below the blob. Let me try this.
Actually, I realize I should just try moving one square towards the blob and see what happens. Let me click (37, 34) a few times and observe.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key insight from initial frame: there was a diagonal gray path from blue blob to bottom-left
# Initial state had: blue blob, gray path dots, purple squares, white shape
# After clicking all gray dots, we got 4 magenta squares and a transformed top bar
# 
# Let me try moving squares toward the blob to see if touching it triggers something
# Square at (37, 34) is closest to blob (blob at rows 23-31, cols 29-37)
# Clicking 5 times moves it to (32, 29), just below blob's bottom-left

# Let me batch-click the square at (37, 34) five times to move it near the blob
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 37, 'col': 34},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 33},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 32},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 31},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 30}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking gray path dots created magenta squares; clicking magenta squares moves them up-left by 1"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_blob": "Blue blob, rows 23-31, cols 29-37", "magenta_squares": "2x2 magenta squares that move up-left when clicked"}, "hud_map": {"top_bar": "S, M, p, Y small squares + large Y square", "bottom_bar": "white bar at row 63"}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Click all path dots in order"}, "level_log": {"level1": "16 clicks", "level2": "61 clicks, 4 magenta squares remain"}, "cross_level_notes": "Level 2 initial state had gray diagonal path from blob to bottom-left with purple anchors"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "4 magenta squares at (32,13), (37,34), (53,44), (54,13); blue blob at center; top bar with 4 colors", "goal_model": "HYPOTHESIS: Move squares to touch/overlap blob", "recent_findings": ["Initial state had diagonal gray path, purple squares, white shape"], "open_questions": ["What happens when square touches blob?"], "current_plan": "Move square (37,34) toward blob by 5 clicks", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Top bar clicks", "Bottom bar click", "Blob click", "Empty space clicks", "ACTION7", "Square then top bar", "Top bar then square"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 61 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the initial state of level 2 clearly:
- Top bar: charcoal background (c) with purple (p) 3x3 square at (4,30-32)
- Black background (B)
- Blue blob (b) 59 pixels at rows 11-19, cols 44-52
- Gray (G) 3x3 square at (14,47-49) with 14 G dots forming a diagonal path from (17,46) to (57,6)
- White (W) 5-pixel object at (52,10) - looks like a small shape
- Purple (p) 3x3 square at (58,3-5)
- White (W) bar at row 63, 64 pixels wide
So the initial state had:
1. A blue blob (59 pixels)
2. A gray 3x3 square with a diagonal path of gray dots (14 dots)
3. Two purple 3x3 squares (top and bottom)
4. A white shape near the end of the path
5. A white bar at the bottom
This is very different from what I thought! The initial state didn't have 8 sky blue dots. It had:
- A diagonal path of gray dots
- A blue blob
- Purple squares at top and bottom
- A white shape
Wait, but the current state has:
- A blue blob (69 pixels, different shape!)
- 4 magenta squares
- Top bar with S, M, p, Y colors
- Bottom white bar (28 pixels)
So the level has changed significantly. The initial state had a different layout, and after my actions, it transformed into the current state.
Let me re-examine the initial state:
- Blue blob: 59 pixels, centered around (15, 48)
- Gray path: 3x3 square at (14,47) + 14 dots from (17,46) to (57,6)
- Purple squares: (4,30) and (58,3)
- White shape: (52,10)
- White bar: row 63, 64 pixels
The gray dots form a diagonal path from top-right to bottom-left. The white shape is near the end of the path.
Wait, I think I misread the initial state. Let me look more carefully:
- The blue blob is at rows 11-19, cols 44-52 (top-right area)
- The gray path starts at (14,47) which is inside/near the blue blob
- The path goes: (17,46), (19,44), (21,42), (23,40), (25,38), (27,36), (29,34), (31,32), (33,30), (35,28), (37,26), (39,24), (41,22), (43,20), (45,18), (47,16), (49,14), (51,12), (55,8), (57,6)
- The white shape is at (52,10), which is near the end of the path
- The purple square at (58,3) is near the end of the path
So the initial state had a diagonal path of gray dots from the blue blob to near the bottom-left corner. The white shape and purple square are near the end.
Now, after my actions, the state is:
- Blue blob: 69 pixels, centered around (27, 33)
- 4 magenta squares
- Top bar with S, M, p, Y colors
- Bottom white bar (28 pixels)
This is a completely different layout. The level transformed after I clicked the dots.
Wait, but I clicked sky blue dots in the initial state, not gray dots. Let me re-examine...
Actually, looking at the initial frame segmentation again:
- The dots are gray (G), not sky blue (S)
- There's no sky blue in the initial frame
But in my earlier analysis, I thought the dots were sky blue. Let me check the current frame's top bar:
- S (sky blue) at (1,1)
- M (magenta) at (1,5)
- p (purple) at (1,9)
- Y (yellow) at (1,13)
These are new objects that appeared after I clicked the gray dots. So the top bar colors are related to the initial state somehow.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me think about what happened:
1. Initial state: gray path from blue blob to bottom-left
2. I clicked the gray dots (thinking they were sky blue)
3. Each click created a magenta square
4. After clicking all dots, 4 magenta squares remained
5. The top bar changed to show S, M, p, Y colors
Wait, but the initial state also had a different top bar:
- Charcoal (c) background
- Purple (p) 3x3 square at (4,30)
And the current state has:
- Black (B) 48-pixel region at top-left with S, M, p, Y squares
- Charcoal (c) 560-pixel region with yellow (Y) 16-pixel square
So the top bar completely changed. This suggests that the level transformed after I clicked the dots.
Let me think about what the level is asking:
1. Initial state: gray path from blue blob to bottom-left
2. Click the gray dots along the path
3. Each click creates a magenta square
4. After clicking all dots, the level transforms to a new state with 4 magenta squares and a new top bar
5. The goal is to do something with the 4 magenta squares
But what? The squares move up-left when clicked. Maybe the goal is to move them to specific positions?
Looking at the initial state's purple squares:
- (4, 30): top-right area
- (58, 3): bottom-left area
And the white shape at (52, 10).
Maybe the goal is to move the magenta squares to match the positions of the purple squares and white shape? But the purple squares are at (4, 30) and (58, 3), and the white shape is at (52, 10). The magenta squares are at (32, 13), (37, 34), (53, 44), (54, 13).
Hmm, (54, 13) is close to (52, 10). If I click it twice, it moves to (52, 11), which is close to (52, 10). But not exact.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial state. The gray path had 14 dots (plus the 3x3 square). I clicked all of them, and 4 magenta squares remained. But the initial state also had other objects: blue blob, purple squares, white shape.
Maybe the 4 magenta squares correspond to the 4 "anchor" objects in the initial state:
1. Blue blob
2. Purple square (top)
3. Purple square (bottom)
4. White shape
And the goal is to move the magenta squares to match the positions of these anchor objects?
But the anchor objects are no longer visible in the current state. The top bar changed, and the purple squares and white shape are gone.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me try a different approach.
Looking at the current state:
- 4 magenta squares at (32, 13), (37, 34), (53, 44), (54, 13)
- Blue blob at center
- Top bar with S, M, p, Y colors
- Bottom white bar (28 pixels)
The top bar has 4 colors: S, M, p, Y. There are 4 magenta squares. Maybe each square corresponds to one color, and I need to move them to positions that match the colors?
But all squares are magenta, and I can't change their color. So this doesn't make sense.
Wait, let me look at the initial state's top bar again:
- Purple (p) 3x3 square at (4, 30)
And the current state's top bar:
- S, M, p, Y small squares + large Y square
The purple square at (4, 30) in the initial state is now a large yellow square at (3, 30) in the current state. So the top bar changed.
Actually, I think the level has two phases:
1. Phase 1: Click the gray dots along the path (I did this)
2. Phase 2: Do something with the 4 magenta squares
For phase 2, the goal might be to:
- Move the squares to specific positions
- Move the squares until they disappear
- Move the squares until they form a pattern
Let me try moving all 4 squares to the top-left corner and see if the level completes. The square at (32, 13) is closest. Let me click it until it goes off the board.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me look at the positions of the 4 squares:
- (32, 13): row 32, col 13
- (37, 34): row 37, col 34
- (53, 44): row 53, col 44
- (54, 13): row 54, col 13
Notice that (32, 13) and (54, 13) have the same column (13). And (37, 34) and (53, 44) have different columns.
What if the goal is to move the squares so they all have the same column? Or the same row?
For same column:
- (32, 13) and (54, 13) already have col 13
- (37, 34) needs to move left by 21 clicks to reach col 13, but that also moves it up by 21 to row 16
- (53, 44) needs to move left by 31 clicks to reach col 13, but that also moves it up by 31 to row 22
After moving:
- (32, 13): stays at (32, 13)
- (54, 13): stays at (54, 13)
- (37, 34) after 21 clicks: (16, 13)
- (53, 44) after 31 clicks: (22, 13)
So they would all be in column 13, at rows 16, 22, 32, 54. That's a vertical line.
But 21 + 31 = 52 clicks. That's a lot.
Actually, let me think about this differently. What if the goal is to move the squares until they form a 2x2 block (all 4 squares overlapping)?
For (32, 13) and (37, 34) to overlap:
- (32, 13) after n clicks: (32-n, 13-n)
- (37, 34) after m clicks: (37-m, 34-m)
- For overlap: 32-n = 37-m and 13-n = 34-m
- m = n + 5 and m = n + 21
- Contradiction
So they can't overlap. This hypothesis doesn't work.
Let me try yet another idea. What if the goal is to click the squares in a specific order, and the order is determined by the top bar colors?
The top bar has: S, M, p, Y (left to right).
- S = sky blue
- M = magenta
- p = purple
- Y = yellow
The squares are all magenta. Maybe I need to click them in the order of the top bar colors, matching the square's position to the color's position?
But all squares are magenta, so they all match M. This doesn't help.
OK, let me try a completely different hypothesis. What if the goal is to move the squares so they touch the blue blob?
The blue blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37.
- (32, 13): row 32 is just below the blob, col 13 is far left
- (37, 34): row 37 is below the blob, col 34 is within the blob's column range
- (53, 44): far below and right
- (54, 13): far below and left
For (37, 34) to touch the blob:
- Click once: (36, 33)
- Click again: (35, 32)
- Click again: (34, 31)
- Click again: (33, 30)
- Click again: (32, 29)
- Click again: (31, 28) - row 31 is the blob's bottom edge, col 28 is just left of the blob's left edge (29)
So after 6 clicks, (37, 34) would be at (31, 28), which is adjacent to the blob's bottom-left corner (31, 29). Let me try this and see if anything special happens when a square touches the blob.
Actually, let me first check if any square is already adjacent to the blob.
- Blob boundary: rows 23-31, cols 29-37
- (32, 13): row 32, col 13 - not adjacent (row 32 is below row 31, but col 13 is far from col 29)
- (37, 34): row 37, col 34 - not adjacent (row 37 is below row 31)
- (53, 44): not adjacent
- (54, 13): not adjacent
None of the squares are adjacent to the blob. Let me move (37, 34) towards the blob and see what happens.
Actually, (37, 34) is at row 37, col 34. The blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37. So (37, 34) is below the blob (row 37 > row 31) and within the blob's column range (29 <= 34 <= 37).
If I click (37, 34) 6 times:
- After 1: (36, 33)
- After 2: (35, 32)
- After 3: (34, 31)
- After 4: (33, 30)
- After 5: (32, 29)
- After 6: (31, 28)
At (31, 28), the square would be at row 31 (blob's bottom edge) and col 28 (just left of blob's left edge 29). So it would be diagonally adjacent to the blob.
Let me try clicking (37, 34) 5 times to get it to (32, 29), which is directly below the blob's bottom-left corner (31, 29).
Actually wait, let me reconsider. The blob's boundary is [[23, 31], [23, 35], [25, 37], [29, 37], [31, 35], [31, 31], [29, 29], [25, 29]]. So the blob occupies:
- Rows 23-31
- Cols 29-37 (approximately)
The square at (37, 34) is a 2x2 square occupying cells (37, 34), (37, 35), (38, 34), (38, 35).
If I click it 5 times, it moves to (32, 29), occupying cells (32, 29), (32, 30), (33, 29), (33, 30).
The blob's bottom edge is at row 31, so (32, 29) is just below the blob. Let me try this.
Actually, I realize I should just try moving one square towards the blob and see what happens. Let me click (37, 34) a few times and observe.

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key insight from initial frame: there was a diagonal gray path from blue blob to bottom-left
# Initial state had: blue blob, gray path dots, purple squares, white shape
# After clicking all gray dots, we got 4 magenta squares and a transformed top bar
# 
# Let me try moving squares toward the blob to see if touching it triggers something
# Square at (37, 34) is closest to blob (blob at rows 23-31, cols 29-37)
# Clicking 5 times moves it to (32, 29), just below blob's bottom-left

# Let me batch-click the square at (37, 34) five times to move it near the blob
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 37, 'col': 34},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 33},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 32},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 31},
        {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 30}])
</parameter>

{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking gray path dots created magenta squares; clicking magenta squares moves them up-left by 1"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_blob": "Blue blob, rows 23-31, cols 29-37", "magenta_squares": "2x2 magenta squares that move up-left when clicked"}, "hud_map": {"top_bar": "S, M, p, Y small squares + large Y square", "bottom_bar": "white bar at row 63"}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Click all path dots in order"}, "level_log": {"level1": "16 clicks", "level2": "61 clicks, 4 magenta squares remain"}, "cross_level_notes": "Level 2 initial state had gray diagonal path from blob to bottom-left with purple anchors"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "4 magenta squares at (32,13), (37,34), (53,44), (54,13); blue blob at center; top bar with 4 colors", "goal_model": "HYPOTHESIS: Move squares to touch/overlap blob", "recent_findings": ["Initial state had diagonal gray path, purple squares, white shape"], "open_questions": ["What happens when square touches blob?"], "current_plan": "Move square (37,34) toward blob by 5 clicks", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Top bar clicks", "Bottom bar click", "Blob click", "Empty space clicks", "ACTION7", "Square then top bar", "Top bar then square"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
